summary: Round 1 findings 1 and 2 are resolved with tight, directional assertions; finding 3 is deferred on sound grounds. No regressions: every Round 1 assertion is preserved; the new assertions sit alongside existing ones (additive, not replacing). Test count is stable at 7 (CrashHandlerTest) + 8 (CrashStateTest) = 15. Litmus test for the two tightened cases: if production regressed to `TRUNC_MARKER + head`, `content.trimEnd().endsWith("[truncated at 100 KB]")` would fail; if `throwable.message.orEmpty()` were replaced with `.toString()`, both `content.contains("\nmessage: \n")` and `assertFalse(content.contains("message: null"))` would fail. Both tightened tests now kill the mutation they are named for. Finding 3 deferral is accepted — reflection on imports is not directly observable at runtime (imports are erased at compile time); scanning declaredFields/declaredMethods for `kotlinx.coroutines.*` types would not catch an added `CoroutineScope` field unless that field were public and retained after R8, and the production file verifiably contains no kotlinx.coroutines imports today. The suspend-parameter check enforces Decision 10's load-bearing contract (no suspend on the handler surface), and a source-level grep smoke catches the import — two layers is enough for a low-severity guardrail.

verification.finding_1_evidence: CrashHandlerTest.kt:128-131 now asserts content.trimEnd().endsWith("[truncated at 100 KB]") — marker is directionally anchored to end-of-file. A regression placing the marker at the head would fail this assertion. All Round 1 assertions (size-band window, startsWith record-header anchor, head contains RuntimeException) are preserved.
verification.finding_2_evidence:
CrashHandlerTest.kt:156-174 — test renamed from nullMessageException_doesNotCrash to nullMessageException_rendersEmptyMessageNotLiteralNull. Asserts content.contains("
message: 
") (empty-line invariant) and assertFalse(content.contains("message: null")) (no literal-'null' fallback). Production writes `append("message: ").append(message).append('
')` via `throwable.message.orEmpty()`, so with a null-message exception the emitted line is exactly `message: 
`. The preceding line is `ExceptionType: ...
`, so `
message: 
` matches. Round 1 assertions (callCount==1, crashLog.exists()) preserved.
verification.finding_3_rationale: Acceptable. Kotlin imports are not retained at runtime (erased at compile time), so reflection cannot observe them. The suspend-parameter check via kotlin.coroutines.Continuation on declaredMethods.parameterTypes enforces the load-bearing half of Decision 10 — no suspend on the handler's public surface. A field scan (declaredFields.map { it.type.name }) would add marginal value but introduces R8/obfuscation fragility and wouldn't catch non-field couplings (local-variable-only usages, private helper methods). The production file CrashHandler.kt today has zero kotlinx.coroutines imports, confirmed by read. A source-level grep in CI is the right tool for import-level guards; reflection is the wrong tool.
verification.test_count_detail: CrashHandlerTest: 7 @Test methods (writesCrashLogBeforeKiller, deletesDismissedFlagOnNewCrash, repeatedCrashes_overwriteNotAppend, handlerInternalFailure_killsOnce, stacktraceOver100KB_truncationMarkerAtEnd, handlerShape_noSuspendCallsNoCoroutineImports, nullMessageException_rendersEmptyMessageNotLiteralNull). CrashStateTest: 8 @Test methods (crashLogExists_noDismissed_flowTrue, bothExist_flowFalse, neitherExists_flowFalse, markDismissed_flipsToFalseAndCreatesFlag, clear_deletesBothFiles, refresh_rereadsAfterExternalChange, freshInstall_noLogsDir_flowFalseNoException, dismissedThenNewCrash_reappears). Total: 15. Matches Round 1 count exactly — no tests dropped, no tests added.
verification.regressions_detail: No assertion was removed or loosened. Finding 1 fix is purely additive (added one endsWith anchor alongside existing contains/size-band/startsWith assertions). Finding 2 fix is purely additive (added two assertions alongside existing callCount and exists() checks). The test rename is behavior-preserving; the new name correctly advertises the tightened contract.
